name: Pandas
what_it_does: Python data manipulation and analysis library providing DataFrame and Series data structures for loading, transforming, filtering, aggregating, and exporting tabular data from CSV, Excel, SQL, JSON, and other formats.
use_cases: Loading and preprocessing tabular datasets (CSV, Excel, Parquet) before feeding them into agent analysis pipelines
Performing data cleaning and transformation tasks — deduplication, null handling, type coercion — as agent tool operations
Aggregating and summarizing structured data to produce statistics that agents can reason over or report
Joining and merging multiple data sources (SQL-like joins) to enrich agent context with combined datasets
Exporting transformed data to downstream formats (Parquet, CSV, JSON) for agent-to-agent data handoff
not_for: Large datasets that exceed RAM — use Polars, Dask, or Spark for out-of-core and distributed processing
Real-time streaming data pipelines — Pandas is batch-oriented; use Faust, Kafka, or Spark Streaming instead
Pure numeric array computation without tabular structure — NumPy is more appropriate and faster
best_when: You need to manipulate tabular data in Python with a rich ecosystem of I/O connectors and the widest community support, and your dataset fits comfortably in RAM.
avoid_when: Your dataset exceeds available RAM or you need multi-threaded parallel processing — Polars is significantly faster and more memory-efficient for those scenarios.

auth.notes: Library — no auth required to install or use. Credentials for remote data sources (S3, GCS, SQL) are handled by the respective connection libraries, not Pandas itself.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Free and open source — BSD licensed, no usage limits
pricing.notes: Pandas is BSD-3-Clause licensed open source, maintained by NumFOCUS-sponsored contributors. Completely free with no commercial restrictions.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Pandas error messages are generally clear for shape mismatches and type errors but can be verbose with full traceback stacks. KeyError for missing columns is descriptive. Some errors (chained indexing SettingWithCopyWarning) are warnings rather than errors, which agents may silently ignore while producing incorrect results.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: DataFrame operations are pure functions when not using in-place modifications (inplace=True). Agents should prefer returning new DataFrames over mutating existing ones to ensure repeatable pipelines.
known_agent_gotchas: SettingWithCopyWarning is a warning not an error — agents silently produce incorrect results when chained indexing modifies a copy instead of the original; always use .loc/.iloc for assignments
DataFrame.apply() with Python functions is slow for large datasets — agents writing transform pipelines should prefer vectorized operations or use Polars for performance-sensitive workloads
Integer columns with NaN values are silently upcast to float64 — agents doing integer arithmetic on nullable columns will get floats unless using nullable integer dtype (pd.Int64Dtype())
pd.read_csv() infers dtypes which can be wrong (e.g., reading ZIP codes as integers, dropping leading zeros) — agents should specify dtype= explicitly for data integrity
Pandas 2.0 introduced Copy-on-Write behavior changes and removed deprecated APIs from 1.x — agent code using older patterns (e.g., append(), iteritems()) will fail on Pandas 2.x

security_score_components.security_notes: Pandas itself poses minimal security risk as a local library. Risk surfaces: reading untrusted Pickle files (pd.read_pickle) can execute arbitrary code — agents must never load pickles from untrusted sources. Excel files with macros can be vectors; use engine='openpyxl' and avoid macro execution. SQL injection is possible if constructing query strings with user input rather than parameterized queries.
